title: Getting started with heatindexbr
text: Municipality names heatindexbr follow IBGE convention: uppercase without accents. Use hi_search() find correct name: Important: Always use name_muni value returned hi_search() calling hi_municipality(). example, "MOSSORO", "Mossoró".
code: # Case-insensitive partial match hi_search("Mossoro", state = "RN") #>     code_muni name_muni abbrev_state #> 500   2408003   MOSSORO           RN hi_search("Campina Grande", state = "PB") #>     code_muni      name_muni abbrev_state #> 904   2504009 CAMPINA GRANDE           PB

what: Extracting Heat Index for a municipality
title: Getting started with heatindexbr
text: hi_municipality() downloads raster (cached first use), crops municipality boundary, returns summary statistics NOAA classification: noaa_class column uses standard NOAA Heat Index thresholds: can retrieve four synoptic hours leaving hour = NULL:
code: result <- hi_municipality("MOSSORO", state = "RN", hour = "15h") result #>   code_muni name_muni abbrev_state     mean hour year resolution #> 1   2408003   MOSSORO           RN 34.22216  15h 2025     annual #>        noaa_class #> 1 Extreme caution hi_municipality("MOSSORO", state = "RN", hour = NULL)

what: Downloading and plotting the full raster
title: Getting started with heatindexbr
text: hi_download() returns SpatRaster (terra package). raster downloaded cached user data directory: Pass raster directly hi_plot() ready--use map:
code: r <- hi_download(year = 2025, hour = "15h") #> ✔ Using cached: #> /home/runner/.cache/R/heatindexbr/IC_2025_annual_all.tif r #> class       : SpatRaster #> size        : 1983, 1299, 1  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) #> resolution  : 999.7408, 999.9794  (x, y) #> extent      : 5799573, 7098237, 7714924, 9697883  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) #> coord. ref. : SIRGAS 2000 / Brazil Polyconic (EPSG:5880) #> source      : IC_2025_annual_all.tif #> name        :    IC_15h #> min value   : 20.407417 #> max value   : 36.131783 hi_plot(r,         title = "Annual mean Heat Index — 15h (2025)",         hour  = "15h",         year  = 2025) #> <SpatRaster> resampled to 501000 cells.

title: Getting started with heatindexbr
text: sf object (e.g. watershed, state boundary, study area), use hi_shape():
code: library(sf)  # Example: using your own polygon my_area <- st_read("my_area.gpkg")  result <- hi_shape(my_area, hour = "15h") result$stats   # data.frame with mean, min, max, noaa_class result$raster  # clipped SpatRaster

title: Getting started with heatindexbr
text: underlying raster (IC_2025_annual_all.tif) stored EPSG:5880 (SIRGAS 2000 / Brazil Polyconic) — appropriate projected CRS Brazil. use WGS84 version, clipping artifact eastern boundary. See vignette("methodology") full details interpolation method.

code: citation("heatindexbr")

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: Heat Index (IC) computed using Rothfusz (1990) regression equation, combines air temperature (T, °C) relative humidity (RH, %) single thermal comfort index. Wind speed considered, makes index tractable regional-scale mapping homogeneous wind data unavailable. Four synoptic hours selected (00h, 09h, 15h, 21h local time, UTC-3) capture full thermal cycle typical day: nocturnal minimum, morning warming, afternoon peak, evening cooling.

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: Weather stations: INMET automatic stations, 2025 (annual means). completeness threshold 75% applied, resulting 59 73 stations per hour depending data availability. 15h hour (18h UTC) achieves largest network: 73 stations. Covariates used best models:

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: Fifteen spatial interpolation methods compared across four synoptic hours using leave-one-cross-validation (LOOCV). candidate methods included kriging external drift (KDE) various covariate combinations, ordinary kriging, linear regression (coordinates altitude), Random Forest, RF + kriging residuals, IDW, thin-plate splines. Winning method hour: covariates selected vary hour, reflecting distinct physical processes: NDVI contributes night (21h, 00h): local land-surface processes Caatinga biome modulate nocturnal temperature synoptic forcing. ERA5 dominates 15h: regional synoptic forcing drives daytime temperature gradients strongly local vegetation effects. Distance coastline relevant night, sea-breeze effects radiative cooling create clear coastal gradient.

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: Random Forest achieved high R² standard LOOCV. However, block spatial cross-validation (spatial folds) revealed substantial spatial leakage: KDE R² remained stable across protocols. interpret KDE genuinely capturing spatial structure Heat Index, RF partially memorising local neighbourhood station.

what: Why indirect interpolation (T + RH to IC) was rejected
title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: alternative workflow interpolate temperature humidity separately, compute IC interpolated fields. approach tested rejected: introduced systematic bias +0.246°C 15h relative direct IC interpolation. direct approach used throughout.

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: 15h hour, residuals KDE model subjected two tests: Variogram analysis: fitted models (spherical, exponential, Gaussian) converged pure nugget effect: Sill = 0, Nugget approximately 1.05. means residual spatial autocorrelation remains fitting, indicating model absorbed systematic spatial structure. Moran’s : = -0.027, p = 0.864. Residuals spatially random. Together, results confirm KDE model well-specified residual variance reflects local measurement noise, unmodelled spatial patterns.

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: Partial correlations computed sample 100,000 prediction-grid pixels confirm altitude ERA5 capture independent physical processes: IC × ERA5 | altitude = 0.977 (p < 0.001) IC × altitude | ERA5 = -0.929 (p < 0.001) Simple correlations comparison: IC × ERA5 = 0.933, IC × altitude = -0.778, altitude × ERA5 = -0.529. raster redundant elevation model; ERA5 explains nearly remaining variance altitude controlled , confirming thermodynamic basis interpolation.

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: concern sparse station networks prediction errors increase monotonically distance nearest station. correlation LOOCV absolute error distance nearest neighbour station -0.109 (non-significant). Prediction error grow distance. largest errors occur 33 55 km range, corresponding coastal Agreste zones steep gradients. Interior Semiarid areas show smaller errors genuine climatic homogeneity, stations closer. Consequently, distance--station raster interpreted descriptive map monitoring network, predictive confidence interval.

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: use WGS84 version (IC_2025_annual_all_WGS84.tif). reprojection artefact clips eastern boundary domain. EPSG:5880 version correct product.

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: Annual mean Heat Index 15h cross-referenced 2022 IBGE Census population data (31,035,363 inhabitants across 1,477 municipalities): Annual means represent conservative lower bound exposure. Daily peak values specific months can exceed IC > 41°C. Analysis extremes reserved forthcoming publication.

title: Methodology: why trust the raster?
text: Rothfusz, L. P. (1990). heat index equation (, ever wanted know heat index). NWS Technical Attachment SR 90-23. National Weather Service, Fort Worth, TX.

title: Primeiros passos com o heatindexbr
text: Os nomes de municípios heatindexbr seguem o padrão IBGE: maiúsculas sem acentos. Use hi_search() para encontrar o nome correto: Atenção: sempre utilize o valor da coluna name_muni retornado por hi_search() ao chamar hi_municipality(). Por exemplo, "MOSSORO" e não "Mossoró". Esta é uma limitação padrão de nomenclatura IBGE; função hi_search() existe exatamente para facilitar essa conversão.
code: # Busca parcial, sem distinção de maiúsculas hi_search("Mossoro", state = "RN") #>     code_muni name_muni abbrev_state #> 500   2408003   MOSSORO           RN hi_search("Campina Grande", state = "PB") #>     code_muni      name_muni abbrev_state #> 904   2504009 CAMPINA GRANDE           PB

what: Extração do Índice de Calor para um município
title: Primeiros passos com o heatindexbr
text: hi_municipality() baixa o raster (armazenado em cache na primeira vez), recorta para o limite municipal e retorna estatísticas resumidas com classificação NOAA: coluna noaa_class utiliza os limites oficiais da escala NOAA: Para obter todos os quatro horários sinóticos de uma vez, use hour = NULL:
code: resultado <- hi_municipality("MOSSORO", state = "RN", hour = "15h") resultado #>   code_muni name_muni abbrev_state     mean hour year resolution #> 1   2408003   MOSSORO           RN 34.22216  15h 2025     annual #>        noaa_class #> 1 Extreme caution hi_municipality("MOSSORO", state = "RN", hour = NULL)

what: Download e visualização do raster completo
title: Primeiros passos com o heatindexbr
text: hi_download() retorna um SpatRaster (pacote terra). O raster é baixado uma única vez e reutilizado cache nas chamadas seguintes: Passe o raster diretamente para hi_plot() para gerar o mapa:
code: r <- hi_download(year = 2025, hour = "15h") #> ✔ Using cached: #> /home/runner/.cache/R/heatindexbr/IC_2025_annual_all.tif r #> class       : SpatRaster #> size        : 1983, 1299, 1  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) #> resolution  : 999.7408, 999.9794  (x, y) #> extent      : 5799573, 7098237, 7714924, 9697883  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) #> coord. ref. : SIRGAS 2000 / Brazil Polyconic (EPSG:5880) #> source      : IC_2025_annual_all.tif #> name        :    IC_15h #> min value   : 20.407417 #> max value   : 36.131783 hi_plot(r,         title = "IC médio anual — 15h (2025)",         hour  = "15h",         year  = 2025) #> <SpatRaster> resampled to 501000 cells.

title: Primeiros passos com o heatindexbr
text: Se você tiver um objeto sf próprio (bacia hidrográfica, limite estadual, área de estudo), use hi_shape():
code: library(sf)  minha_area <- st_read("minha_area.gpkg")  resultado <- hi_shape(minha_area, hour = "15h") resultado$stats   # data.frame com mean, min, max, noaa_class resultado$raster  # SpatRaster recortado

title: Primeiros passos com o heatindexbr
text: O raster base (IC_2025_annual_all.tif) está em EPSG:5880 (SIRGAS 2000 / Brazil Polyconic) — o SRC projetado adequado para o Brasil. versão em WGS84 possui um artefato de recorte na borda leste e não deve ser utilizada. Consulte vignette("methodology") para detalhes completos sobre o método de interpolação.

Local cache directory. overwrite Logical. Re-download even cached? Default FALSE. quiet Logical. Suppress messages? Default FALSE.

title: Extract Heat Index for municipalities of the Brazilian Semiarid — hi_municipality
text: municipality Character integer. Municipality name (uppercase, accents) 7-digit IBGE code. Use hi_search find valid names. Names follow IBGE uppercase standard without accents (e.g. "MOSSORO" "Mossoro" "Mossoro"). state Character. Two-letter state abbreviation (e.g. "RN"). hour Character NULL. Local hour: "00h", "09h", "15h" (default), "21h", NULL hours. year Integer. Year (default 2025). resolution Character. Temporal resolution (default "annual"). month Integer. Month (1-12) resolution = "monthly". day Integer. Day (1-365) resolution = "daily". stat Character vector. Statistics: "mean" (default), "min", "max", "median", "sd", "q25", "q75". return_raster Logical. Also return clipped SpatRaster? cache_dir Character. Cache directory.
